relationship_to_restored_text: Interpretive contemplative layer aligned by verse. This does not replace the source-audited restored translation.

verses.mystical_translation: Eliphaz begins his second speech.
He hears Job’s wisdom as wind.
He calls Job’s words useless.
He accuses Job of weakening reverence before the Divine.
He claims Job’s own guilt is teaching his mouth.
He says Job’s lips condemn him.
He mocks any claim to firstborn wisdom.
He asks whether Job has listened in the divine council.
He insists the friends know as much as Job.
He appeals to age and tradition.
He frames their counsel as divine comfort.
He asks why Job’s heart and eyes are storming.
He accuses Job of turning his spirit against God.
He repeats the old claim: no human born of woman is clean or righteous.
Even heavenly ones are not clean enough, he says.
How much less corrupt humanity drinking injustice like water.
Eliphaz offers what he claims to have seen.
He appeals again to the sayings of the wise and fathers.
He invokes an older, uncontaminated tradition.
He paints the wicked as writhing all their days.
Dread sounds in their ears even in peace.
Darkness and sword wait for them.
They wander for bread beneath a ready day of darkness.
Distress and anguish overpower them.
Because, Eliphaz says, they raised a hand against Shaddai.
They run stiff-necked against the Divine.
Their arrogance is pictured as swollen self-indulgence.
Their dwellings become ruins.
Their wealth will not endure.
Darkness, flame, and breath end their growth.
Emptiness is their exchange.
Their branch fails before its day.
Their fruit falls unripe.
The godless company becomes barren, and bribery’s tents burn.
They conceive trouble, birth harm, and prepare deceit.
